articles.title: Quantum chemical modeling, molecular docking, and ADMET evaluation of imidazole phenothiazine hybrids.
articles.abstract: Cancer is one of the biggest challenges for health concerns in the world. There are so many drugs available, but they have a lack of specificity, poor safety, side effects, and the development of resistance. Therefore, there is an urgent need for much safer and more targeted anticancer treatments. Nitrogen-containing heterocycles play an important role in the development of drugs. Recently, imidazole and phenothiazine rings are well known for their antiproliferative and anticancer activities. This study employs the molecular hybridisation method to link these bioactive scaffolds and develop novel N-substituted imidazole-phenothiazine (N-IPTZ) hybrids. All the synthesised hybrids were characterised by using analytical techniques such as 1 H-NMR, 13 C-NMR, mass spectrum, and FT-IR. Furthermore, the DFT analysis under the B3LYP/6-311G(d, p) level in gas phase to optimise and correlate the structures of the synthesised hybrids was also performed. The optimised structure was used to determine the energies of frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO-LUMO), quantum chemical descriptors (QCD), and molecular electrostatic potentials (MEP). Additionally, in silico approaches such as ADMET, BOILED-Egg, and bioactivity radar were also performed to evaluate the oral bioavailability of the synthesised hybrids. Molecular docking and MD simulation studies were also conducted to assess the interaction profile of the synthesised hybrids with cancer target receptors like EGFR, IGF, VEGFR1, VEGFR2, and PARP-2. It was found through docking studies that the synthesised N-IPTZ(a-c) hybrids might interact with amino acids such as GLY695, SER696, GLY697, ALA698, PHE699, LYS721, GLY772, CYS773, THR766, GLN767, LEU768, MET769, ARG817, ASN818, and THR830. Additionally, it reveals hydrogen bonding with ASP831, with binding energies of - 7.23, - 6.11, and - 5.93 kcal/mol. Moreover, all the synthesised hybrids were also analysed for their anti-cancer activity against the human liver cancer cell line (HepG2) by MTT assay. Obtained results revealed that N-IPTZ(c) exhibited anticancer activity with an IC 50 value of 35.3 µg/mL.

articles.full_text: The World Health Organization recognized that cancer was the second-highest cause of death worldwide in 2018, accounting for over 9.6 million deaths and about 18 million reported cases. In the United States alone, around 2.0 million cancer cases were diagnosed in 2023 1 , 2 . In general, men tend to report lung, prostate, colorectal, stomach, and liver cancer, while women report colorectal, lung, cervical, and thyroid cancer 3 . Breast cancer, which affects an estimated 297,790 women and 2,800 men is the most common malignancy to receive a diagnosis whereas prostate cancer was closely the leading diagnosis among men, with an expected 288,300 cases 3 , 4 and lung and bronchus cancer is the third most diagnosed, with approximately 238,340 new cases 5 , 6 . Researchers have examined various aspects of these cancers, focusing on the development of compounds for the treatment of cancer 7 . Therefore, researchers have recognized heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen atoms as most suitable category of organic chemistry with extensive significance in various disciplines of physiological, biological, and medicinal science 8 – 10 . Nitrogen-based anti-cancer drugs such as Doxorubicin 11 , Thioridazine 11 , Decarbazine, Nicotinib, Axitinib, Cediranib, Sunitinib, and Linifanib hold significant importance. 6 , 12 Similarly, some recently reported phenothiazine and azole derivatives (I-VII) 13 – 17 also display potential cytotoxic activity (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 Some biologically active phenothiazine and imidazole derivatives. ( a ) Are nitrogen-based heterocycles that incorporate commercially available drugs, such as Decarbazine (DACA), Doxorubicin (DOXO), Thioridazine (THIO), Erlotinib, Nicotinib, Axitinib, Sunitinib, Linifanib, Cediranib ( b ). Recent studies have identified the azole/phenothiazine derivatives (I to VII) as potential scaffolds against HepG2 and EGFR. Some biologically active phenothiazine and imidazole derivatives. ( a ) Are nitrogen-based heterocycles that incorporate commercially available drugs, such as Decarbazine (DACA), Doxorubicin (DOXO), Thioridazine (THIO), Erlotinib, Nicotinib, Axitinib, Sunitinib, Linifanib, Cediranib ( b ). Recent studies have identified the azole/phenothiazine derivatives (I to VII) as potential scaffolds against HepG2 and EGFR. Consequently, a thorough study of all U.S. FDA-approved drugs showed that 59% of these small molecules contain nitrogen-containing heterocycles 9 . The five-membered heterocyclic molecule imidazole has been of a great interest as it has a strong polar effect and functions as an acid-base and hydrogen donor. Imidazole is a form of 1,3-diazole, and its derivatives have already possess many medicinal uses, including painkillers, antifungals 18 , antihypertensives, anti-obesity drugs, antitumor drugs, antivirals, anthelmintics, antitubercular 19 , anti-ulcer, antihistamines, anti-inflammatory 20 , antidepressants, antidiabetics, anticonvulsants, anti-allergens, antirheumatics, anti-asthmatics, alpha-blockers, antiprotozoal agents, anti-ageing products, and anti-coagulants 21 , 22 . Currently, some commercial available drugs such as VERU-111 23 , BZML 24 , and Nilotinib 25 are broadly known imidazole-based nitrogen-containing heterocycles. VERU-111, a commercial drug candidate, exhibits potential anti-proliferative efficacy against TNBC cell lines such as MDA-MB-231 and MDA-MB-468. BZML inhibits tubulin polymerization and damages DNA, which makes it very effective at inhibiting colorectal cancer cells 26 . Nilotinib, classified as a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, obstructs abnormal signals from blood proteins that contribute to the formation of leukaemia cancer cells 25 . So, this became the basis to choose imidazole and its derivatives as nucleophilic moiety of the hybrid. On the other hand, a well-known class of compounds is phenothiazine, which possesses the 10 H-dibenzo-[b, e]-1,4-thiazine structure that is composed of three rings, including two benzene rings connected by sulphur and a nitrogen atom 27 , 28 . It has three parts, including a water-repelling core, a water-loving side chain that comes from the middle ring ( N -10), and a substituent at the C -2 position 29 , 30 . It is often used as an important building block for making powerful medicines that have a wide range of biological uses, such as anti-inflammatory, antihistaminic, antipsychotic, anticholinergic, antiemetic, sedative, tranquillizer, antimalarial, analgesic, antitubercular, antimicrobial, and anticancer properties 31 – 34 . Notably, studies have shown that phenothiazine-based drugs, such as trifluoperazine (TFP) and thioridazine (THD), inhibit the growth of breast cancer cell lines like SUM159, MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, and SKBR-3. The American Cancer Society (ACS) highlights that numerous FDA-approved EGFR inhibitors, such as afatinib, erlotinib, and osimertinib, are nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2 Schematic representation for designing of N -substituted imidazole-phenothiazine hybrids ( N -IPTZ) along with commercially available phenothiazine and imidazole containing standard drugs. Schematic representation for designing of N -substituted imidazole-phenothiazine hybrids ( N -IPTZ) along with commercially available phenothiazine and imidazole containing standard drugs. Some recent studies reported that the imidazole and phenothiazine derivatives have shown antiproliferative and anticancer activities. Both scaffolds have demonstrated their effectiveness by inhibiting EGFR and inducing cytotoxic inhibition. Therefore, phenothiazine appears to be a suitable scaffold when combined with nitrogenous heterocycles like imidazole and benzimidazole to develop a new nitrogen-containing heterocyclic hybrid. Moreover, the recent studies suggest that acetic acid is a suitable choice as both a catalyst and solvent. Additionally, it significantly contributes to ring opening, enabling the N -substitution process on phenothiazine 35 , 36 . This has inspired our efforts to develop hybrid compounds that incorporate phenothiazine with a nitrogenous scaffold in the presence of acetic acid. To expedite our study, we initially performed in silico and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to evaluate the pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of the designed N -substituted imidazole-phenothiazine hybrids. Using DFT, the structures of the synthesised hybrids were optimised. This study employed benchmark analyses to further optimise the ground-state configurations of the imidazole and phenothiazine hybrid hybrids. The ab initio DFT method, the B3LYP approach with 6–31 + G(d), 6–31 + G(d, p), and 6-311G(d, p) basis sets, was utilised for optimisations. Chemical shifts and vibrational frequencies were estimated using the 6-311G(d, p) basis set. Additionally, frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) and quantum chemical descriptors (QCD) were calculated to establish correlations with the hybrids’ predicted biological activities. To identify optimal targets and favourable binding scores for assessing the anticancer efficacy of the newly synthesised hybrids, molecular docking and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed against Insulin-like Growth Factors (IGF), Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor Receptors 1 & 2 (VEGFR1, VEGFR2), Epidermal Growth Factor Receptors (EGFR), and Poly (ADP-ribose) Polymerase-2 (PARP-2) 37 – 39 . Additionally, we conducted ADMET profiling and in silico evaluations to assess the drug-likeness of the synthesised hybrids. Figure 3 schematically depicts the step-by-step representation of this study. Fig. 3 A schematic depiction of this study. A schematic depiction of this study. All solvents and reagents used in this study were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA, and were of the utmost quality; they were not subjected to any additional purification processes. TLC was performed on silica gel G (Merck) using 100–400 mesh silica gel plates (GF254), and spots were visualised by iodine vapour. The melting points of the hybrids were taken by Electronics India apparatus. The functional groups were identified using a Bruker-Vector 22 spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm − 1 using potassium bromide pellets or as liquid films. 1 H-NMR (400 MHz) and 13 C-NMR spectra (101 MHz) were recorded on a Bruker Avance spectrometer with CDCl 3 - d as the solvent and tetramethyl silane (TMS) as an internal standard (chemical shifts δ, ppm). Using an ESI Micro mass ZMD 2000 mass spectrometer, electro-spray ionization (ESI) in positive mode, produced mass spectra. In a dry round bottom flask, phenothiazine (1 eq, 2 mmol, 0.39 g) was added to minimum amount of toluene. After mixing for about 15 min, epichlorohydrin (2.4 eq, 4.8 mmol, 374.40 µmL) was added and started refluxing the mixture at 80 °C for about 4–5 h. The reaction was completed after continuously monitoring it with TLC (n-hexane: ethyl acetate = 7:3). Shiny crystals of phenothiazine intermediate were formed 40 . Phenothiazine intermediate (0.1 mmol) and substituted imidazole (0.1 mmol) were taken in a dry round-bottom flask. Acetic acid (4.5 mL) as a solvent as well as a catalyst was added and started refluxing at 80 °C for about 3–4 h. After continuous TLC monitoring, n-hexane and ethyl acetate at 7:3 was emptied into excess distilled water and filtered out the product precipitate after repeatedly washing it with distilled water to remove all acetic acid and yield pure products 35 . Conventional methods such as column chromatography for purification reduce yield, require specific time, and result in significant solvent waste. Considering the points in this study, attempting to develop a reaction where products are obtained through precipitation. After filtering the products to remove acetic acid traces, thoroughly wash the obtained products with distilled water to avoid any impurities and find pure products 35 . molecule are quantitatively described using the numerical values of molecular descriptors (MDs) 47 . A chemical compound’s oral bioavailability is influenced by a variety of MDs, including MW (molecular weight), TPSA (topological polar surface area), RB (rotatable bonds), OHNH (hydrogen bond donors), logP (partition coefficient), and ON (acceptors and donors of hydrogen bonds). These MDs can also explain drugs-likeness of molecules. Currently, numerous rules have been accessible to clarify the potential medical use or druglike characteristics of a compound, such as Veber, Lipinski’s (Ro5), Ghose’s, Muegge’s, and lead likeness rules. ChemDraw-Professional v15.1 and Molinspiration Engine v2022.08 were used to calculate several MDs in the present investigation as a free web software tool 48 , 49 . Drugs’ oral bio-availability can be explained with the help of following rules 50 : Veber’s rule: TPSA (Topological Polar Surface Area) ≤ 140 Å 2 and nRB (Number of Rotatable bonds) ≤ 10 are considered, here 51 . Lipinski’s rule of five: It considers the following parameters: logP ≤ 5, nHD (Number of H-bond Donors) ≤ 10, nHA (Number of H-bond Acceptors) ≤ 5, and MW (Molecular Weight) 500 Da 52 . Ghose’s rule: It includes parameters 160 ≤ MW (Molecular weight) ≤ 480, − 0.4 ≤ logP (Partition Coefficient) ≤ 5.6, 40 ≤ MR (Molar Refractivity) ≤ 130, 20 ≤ Atoms ≤ 70. Muegge’s rule: It includes parameters 200 ≤ MW (Molecular Weight) ≤ 600, 2 ≤ logP (Partition coefficient) ≤ 5, nRing (Number of Rings) ≤ 7, number of carbon atoms > 4, nHet (Number of Heteroatom) > 1, nHA (Number of H-bond Acceptors) ≤ 10, nHD (Number of H-bond Donors) ≤ 5, and golden triangle rule for metabolically stable compounds considers 200 ≤ MW ≤ 50, 2 ≤ logD ≤ 5 53 . Leadlikeness: For lead likeness, a drug candidate molecule must possess nRB < 7, XLOGP3 < 3.5, and MW 250–350 Da 54 . Some of the other in silico parameters also can be described for drug likeliness of the compound such as; BMS (The Bristol–Myers Squibb’s) rule: It removes unwanted and dangerous reactive chemicals and reagents. The alarm NMR rule: Moreover, chemicals that may be promiscuous or reactive are identified using it. It also can differentiate between covalent inhibitors and other suspects that are malicious or harmless just like PAINS filters. Reactivity warnings derived from alarm NMR serve as a valuable marker of potential phase I and II metabolic reactions of various substances. The bioactivity score is another measure of the drug-likeness of a chemical scaffold predicted by Molinspiration v2018.10. BAS determined that the chemical under investigation demonstrates physiological activity, and this activity increases as the given value increases. BAS predicts enzymes, nuclear receptors, kinase inhibitors, GPCR, and iron channel modulators 55 , 56 . ADMET values are easily predicted using in silico tools to understand the potential of a chemical scaffold to cross physiological barriers and intestinal absorption. In this study, the ADMET properties of the synthesised N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids were evaluated with the help of preADMET ( https://preadmet2.bmdrc.kr ) and admetSAR (ecust.edu.cn), which gather details about the ADMET properties of chemical scaffolds from recent studies available on different platforms such as BBB penetration (Blood Brain Barrier Penetration), HIA (Human Intestinal Absorption) caco-2 permeability, HOB (Human Oral-bioavailability), carcinogenicity, EI (Eye Irritation), AMES mutagenesis, hepato-toxicity, TPSA, and AOT (Acute Oral Toxicity) 57 , 58 . TPSA has helped to explain how potential compounds enter cells and cross membranes. Caco-2 cell permeability, a human colon epithelial cancer cell line, studies intestinal barrier molecule crossing. Chemical scaffold oral absorption is assessed using HIA 59 . The distribution of these cells is studied using P-glycoprotein (P-gp). Transdermal drug distribution is assessed by skin permeability (SP). BBB penetration predicts whether the target penetrates the BBB, reducing pharmacological toxicity and side effects or increasing efficacy. Plasma protein binding affects the effectiveness and duration of therapy. Drug safety, persistence, and bio-activation need cytochrome P450 isoforms 2C19, 2D6, 2C9, 1A2 and 3A4. NSAIDs and mildly acidic medicines are metabolised by CYP2C9 with hydrogen-bond acceptors. About half of clinically used drugs interact with CYP3A4. CYP1A2 metabolises aromatic and heterocyclic substances. Most substances with scores between 0.0 and 0.3 inhibit or substrate CYP-metabolizing enzymes, moderate-weak inhibitors, or substrates between 0.4 and 0.7, and non-inhibitors or substrates between 0.8 and 1.0. Drug metabolites are removed in multiple ways whereas drug excretion might be unmodified or metabolised. The conversion of water-insoluble drugs into polar, water-soluble molecules makes them easy to flush. Size and pH affect medication excretion such as basic urine flushes weakly acidic pharmaceuticals more easily than weakly basic drugs and vice versa. The liver can actively release 300 g/mol or more bile-ionized drugs. It has also been found that drug elimination can also depend on genetics and health. Patients with hepatic or renal diseases that disrupt biotransformation channels may have problems flushing medications 41 , 60 . Additionally, ProTox-II uses the worldwide harmonised categorization and labelling system to classify compounds into six toxicity classes (1–6). These classifications are determined by LD 50 values, which are milli-grammes per kilogramme (mg/kg) and represent the median dose of a pharmacological agent required to exterminate 50% of an experimental animal cohort. The six classes are: Class I drugs are lethal if swallowed ranges LD 50 ≤ 5, Class II substances are fatal if LD 50 values ranges 5 < LD 50 ≤ 50, Class III drugs are poisonous if LD 50 values ranges 50 < LD 50 ≤ 300), Class IV drugs are harmful if LD 50 values ranges 300 < LD 50 ≤ 2000, and Class V may pose harm 61 . For a quick determination of the drug-likeness of chemical scaffolds, bioavailability radar is the most valuable analysis. The bioavailability radar was developed utilising six physiological parameters: size, polarity, solubility, lipophilicity, flexibility, and saturation. BOILED-Egg and multiple linear regression models forecast the pharmacokinetic parameters such as GIA (GI absorption), BBB permeability (Blood Brain Barrier permeability), P-gp substrate, CYP450 inhibitors such as 1A2, 2C19, 2C9, 2D6, 3A4, and log K sp (skin permeation) 62 . In this study, both bioavailability radar and BOILED-Egg analysis were performed using SwissADME ( http://www.swissadme.ch ). Molecular docking has made it easier to investigate the interactions between biological macromolecules and ligand molecules. Through molecular docking it can be observed that how well the ligand (small molecule) can bind to the active site (target protein’s specific pocket). The interaction most likely involves a largely non-covalent mode. 63 – 65 For selecting an appropriate pharmacological target for a chemical agent, in silico approaches are quite beneficial. Analysing their interaction is vital to comprehending a chemical entity’s molecular behaviour. The Swiss Target Prediction (STP) tool ( http://swisstargetprediction.ch/ ), which is web-based and free to use, can determine the suitable target for a chemical molecule by utilising SAR and its fragments. To determine the percentage of biological activity that targets the specified intracellular locations, all N -IPTZ(a–c) hybrids were subjected to analysis using the STP server 66 (Fig. 4 ). The STP results show that the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids are effective against kinase, enzyme, and protease. IGF (insulin-like growth factors), VEGFR1, VEGFR2 (vascular endothelial growth factor receptors 1 and 2), EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptors), and PARP-2 (poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase-2) are the key anticancer target proteins. Thus, these target proteins were used to assess the potential of N -substituted imidazole-phenothiazine hybrids as anticancer agents. The protein data bank provided the crystal structures of the apoptotic pathway factors (PDB, http://www.rcsb.org/pdb/home/home.do ), with the corresponding Protein Data Bank (PDB) IDs for these targets 2DSQ, 3HNG, 3VHK, 5CNN, and 5DSY, respectively. CASTp (Computed Atlas of Surface Topography of Proteins; http://sts.bioe.uic.edu/castp/ ), was useful for pocket analysis. Chimaera 1.12 and the default RMSD for additional AMBER force field 14SB were used to optimise energy in target protein 3D structures. Before molecular docking analysis (Fig. 5 ), Kollman charge, polar hydrogen bonds, and margin non-polar hydrogen bonds were added using Auto Dock v4.2.6 67 , 68 . The 2D structures of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids were prepared using ChemDraw Professional version 15.1, followed by MMFF optimisation of the ligand structures in Avogadro v1.2.0. SMILES and PDB files for these ligands were generated using Biovia DSV v2017R. Rotatable bonds and Gasteiger charges were added utilising AutoDock. Standard anticancer drugs, including Dacarbazine (DACA), Doxorubicin (DOXO), and Thioridazine (THIO), were obtained from PubChem ( https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/) 11 . Molecular dynamics modelling is an important tool for understanding how ligands function in living systems. The docking results can be used to figure out important chemical processes, how ligands bind, and what factors affect drugs and how receptors interact with each other. The binding energies linked to the most stable conformation of drug-receptor interactions can be computed using docking programmes. Docking analyses were used to determine the optimal interaction between the ligand and target as well as to confirm that the ligand is positioned correctly in the docking complex by using free binding energy. The highest-scoring model among all the synthesized hybrids, N -IPTZ(c) with EGFR protein, was selected for a 100 ns all-atom molecular dynamics simulation utilising GROMACS. This study offers essential insights into ligand binding energy, kinetics, and the stability of ligand-target complexes through the analysis of MD trajectories utilising GROMACS built-in tools, alongside data comparison including RMSD and Rg. This method enabled the observation and analysis of potential interactions between ligands and protein residues. The binding free energy of the ligand and protein was assessed utilising the GROMACS “g_mmpbsa” module 69 – 71 . Protein binding may impact medication pharmacodynamics and kinetics. This study examined synthesised hybrids using MM/PBSA (Molecular Mechanics/Poisson-Boltzmann Surface Area) calculations with the gmx_mmpbsa tool from the GROMACS software suite to optimise protein-ligand complexes. The relative free binding energy calculations show the effects of different interactions when the ligand binds to the protein receptor and the complex forms {ΔG binding = ΔG complex − (ΔG protein + ΔG ligand )}. The ΔG complex denotes the total free energy of the protein-ligand complex. The ΔG protein and ΔG ligand concurrently denote the total free energy of the dissociated protein and ligand in a solvent. The gmx_mmpbsa tool can compute the energy contribution of each residue to the binding energy and decompose the binding energy. In this study, calculate ΔE MM , ΔG polar , and ΔGnon−polar for each residue separately and subsequently aggregate them to ascertain each residue’s contribution to the binding energy. 69 , 72 – 74 The MTT assay was opted to determine the antiproliferative activity of different synthesised hybrids N -IPTZ(a-c). All the synthesised hybrids were applied for the treatment of the cells, followed by the incubation of 24 h. 0.1% DMSO in media was used for cells in the control group. Media of treated and control cells was removed and washed with 100 µL of PBS. 10 µL of MTT reagent was added to each well, and the microplate was incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. MTT reagent was removed from each well, followed by adding 100 µL DMSO and keeping it for 4 h of incubation. Absorbance values for each treatment in triplicates were recorded using an ELISA reader (xMarkTM Microplate Absorbance Spectrophotometer, BioRad, United States) at a 540 nm wavelength and compared with untreated cells 75 . Dose response inhibition curves in Origin 2018 were used to calculate the IC 50 values. This study performed using a one-pot solvent-free reaction, where 10-(oxiran-2-ylmethyl)-10 H-phenothiazine ( N -IPTZ) was prepared by mixing a solution of phenothiazine and toluene with an appropriate amount of epichlorohydrin while refluxing at 80 °C. Accordingly, N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids were obtained by stirring 10-(oxiran-2-ylmethyl)- 10 H -phenothiazine (1 eq.) with substituted imidazole (1.05 eq.) along with a catalytic amount of acetic acid (1 eq.) to yield the corresponding products (65–75%) and are not yet reported to the best of our knowledge (Scheme 1 ). Synthesis of N -substituted imidiazole-phenothiazine ( N -IPTZ(a-c)) hybrids For this synthesis, reaction optimisation was done by using different solvents such as DMF, methanol, and ethanol; however, trace amounts of products were obtained. Then, as per the literature survey, acetic acid was used for the same reaction as a catalyst as well as the solvent to help in epoxy ring opening and results better yield than other tested solvents. Additionally, using acetic acid provides a solvent-free and efficient approach to develop a series of N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids. The completion of reaction was observed with TLC using n-hexane and ethyl acetate (in 7:3 ratio respectively). The workup of the reaction was done by pouring the reaction mixture into distilled water to obtain the precipitate of the product. Excess water should be used while filtering the products till the smell of acetic acid disappears to avoid by-products. In this study, we used 1 H-NMR, 13 C-NMR, FT-IR, and ESI-MS to figure out the structures of N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids. The IR analysis showed a frequency at 1280 cm −1 that confirmed the formation of an epoxy intermediate of phenothiazine. IR frequencies were observed at 1312 and 1443 cm −1 , which correspond to C=N and C-N stretching respectively. The OH stretching frequency was observed at 3620 cm −1 . The 1 H-NMR tests show that there are two doublets at 5.79 and 6.82 ppm which confirms the presence of OH and imidazole protons. Moreover, peaks of aromatic protons of phenothiazine are observed at 6.53, 6.55, 6.98 ppm. The 13 C-NMR spectra showed an imidazole carbon with a methyl group has a peak at 13.12 ppm and other two imidazole carbons are at 122.71 and 118.38 ppm, respectively. A carbon peak with OH is observed at 77.60 ppm, and aromatic carbon peaks can be seen at 141.86 and 127.43 ppm. In addition, the ESI-MS analysis has also confirmed the mass/charge ratio found at 338.2[M + H] + for C 19 H 19 N 3 OS. Firstly, the reaction between phenothiazine (PTZ) and epichlorohydrin was done at room temperature with K 2 CO 3 in methanol. The experiment was also carried out in various solvents at different temperatures to elevate the product yield. However, 10-(oxiran-2-ylmethyl)-10 H-phenothiazine was prepared by refluxing PTZ with epichlorohydrin in toluene at 80 °C. The shiny crystals of PTZ intermediate with epichlorohydrin were formed after cooling down the mixture to room temperature. Accordingly, procedures for the preparation of N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids were formed after optimising the reaction conditions by using various solvents such as MeOH, EtOH, and DMF at different temperatures (room to reflux) to get the desired products. Interesting results were found at first without the use of any catalyst. Nevertheless, 70–90% product yield was obtained when acetic acid was used as a catalyst as well as a solvent. Acetic acid here helps to deprotonate azoles and forms active nucleophiles which further react with the N -substituted epoxy ring of PTZ, which helps the epoxy ring open to make the final N -substituted heterocycles of PTZ through a ring opening via nucleophilic substitution reaction. Hence, a solvent-free, one-pot-efficient approach was developed to prepare a series of N -IPTZ(a–c) as per the mechanism shown in Fig. 6 . DFT (B3LYP)6-311G(d, p) was used to find the optimum N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids molecular structure. Because the theoretical study is in gas phase and the experimental study is solid phase, the bond length and angle vary somewhat. Therefore, DFT calculations matched experimental results better. Figure 7 ; Table 3 , and Supplementary Tables 2 – 4 illustrate the optimal structure. In this study, C–H (~ 1.10 Å), imidazole ring C–C (~ 1.39 Å), and C–N (1.41–1.47 Å) have shorter average bond lengths in a phenothiazine ring than C–H (~ 1.10 Å), C–C (~ 1.40 Å), and C–N (~ 1.43 Å). These bond lengths go up in order. Due to inductive effect potentials that take away electrons, the hydroxy substitution (C16–O17H) having bond length of approx. 1.42 Å. When inductive effect takes place, it mostly changes the lengths of C–N (C18–N19) bonds (~ 1.46 Å) because the imidazole and indole rings are substituted. It was also found that the bond angles of the aromatic, imidazole, and phenothiazine rings were (~ 118.3–120.8°, ~ 110.–129.3°, and ~ 117.5–122.7°) getting wider because of the ring strain. The vibrational modes of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids, calculated using vacuum-optimized molecules, revealed a total of 114 to 144 normal modes. Analysis of the vibrational spectra confirmed that the molecular configuration was in its lowest energy state, resulting in the visibility of most peaks within the vibrational spectrum 76 . Recorded the calculated spectra derived from an isolated molecule in a vacuum, in contrast to the experimental spectra acquired in the solid state, which allows for multiple interactions. This result demonstrates the ability to explain the experimental structure using the chosen theory level. Functional groups in a molecule can be revealed by vibrational frequencies 77 . This study uses the DFT (B3LYP)6-311G(d, p) level in the gas phase to derive the vibrational frequencies of N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids. In this investigation, the PED analysis of the N -IPTZ hybrids (S. Tables 4 and 5 ) are reported. These observations and calculations revealed that the N -IPTZ hybrids contain phenothiazine and imidazole rings where the O–H stretching is expected to fall around 3200 to 3600 cm − 1 . The theoretical measurements of O–H stretching in the N -IPTZ hybrids showed at 3690, 3682, and 3708 cm − 1 , while the experimental results were at 3640, 3620, and 3633 cm − 1 , respectively 78 . It is possible to find the prominent vibrations of the C–N, C–S, and C=N stretching modes. It is anticipated that the N-C bonds stretching vibrations will fall between the 1000 and 1400 cm − 1 range. The N-C vibrations of N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids have been experimentally observed at frequencies of approximately ~ 1435 cm − 1 , in that order. There have been theoretical vibrations detected at 1236, 1237, 1349, 1356, and 1364 cm − 1 , respectively. The C-S stretching is predicted to occur within the range of 1300 –1000 cm − 1 . The theoretical stretching of N- IPTZ(a-c) hybrids was measured at 670, 679, and 680 cm − 1 , while the experimental stretching was observed at 657, and 656 cm − 1 , respectively 78 . The stretching frequencies of C=N for N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids were successfully correlated, with experimental measurements at 1313, 1381, and 1303 cm − 1 , and theoretical determinations at 1337, 1340, and 1322 cm − 1 . The aromatic groups C–H stretching vibrations are typically found between 3000 and 3200 cm − 1 , depending on the other substituents. Substitutes near the ring do not significantly impact the vibration band. The experimental vibrational spectra of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids revealed the C–H stretching band in the phenothiazine and imidazole rings at 3056, 3054, 2917 cm − 1 , contrasting with the theoretically predicted band at 3068, 3069, and 3170 cm − 1 . 77 , 79 The C=C stretching modes exhibit a very strong peak in N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids between 1400 and 1650 cm − 1 . Experimentally, the stretching vibrations of C=C were observed at 1572 and 1596 cm − 1 . Theoretically, they were observed at 1584, 1584, and 1585 cm −1 78 , 80 . The aliphatic C-H bonds exhibit stretched vibrations in the range of 3000–2850 cm −1 . The N -IPTZ(b-c) hybrids contain the –CH 3 group, which is theoretically expected to be detected at 2928 cm − 1 , however, the experimental spectrum showed it at 3054 cm − 1 . The scaled vibrational frequencies demonstrate a strong correlation with the experimental values, as indicated by the high correlation coefficient. This finding suggests a good agreement between the theoretical predictions and the experimental results (Table 4 ). The N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids primarily contain aromatic C–H, aliphatic C–H, and O–H protons in the presence of phenothiazine and imidazole rings. The phenothiazine ring (C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C8, C9, C11, C12, C13, and C14) and the imidazole ring (C20, C22, and C23) primarily contain the aromatic C–H. Protons in the phenothiazine ring primarily ranged from 6.53 to 6.99 ppm in the experimental and 6.97–7.31 ppm in the calculated spectra for N -IPTZ(a–b) hybrids, whereas they were 6.77–7.00 ppm in the experimental and 6.89–7.06 ppm in the calculated spectra for the N -IPTZ(c) hybrid. The imidazole ring observed at 6.97 and 6.82 ppm whereas calculated at 6.89 and 6.97 ppm, respectively. The protons in the phenothiazine-imidazole linker (phenothiazine-NCH 2 C(OH)HCH 2 N-imidazole) have three different environments and three different chemical shift values. The phenothiazine-NCH 2 protons were mostly observed at 3.78 and 2.10 ppm, respectively and theoretically calculated value found at 3.61, 3.58, and 3.79 ppm, respectively. The C(OH)H proton was mostly observed at 3.49 and 3.78 ppm, respectively and theoretically calculated found at 3.77 and 4.46 ppm, respectively. Similarly, the imidazole-NCH 2 protons were mostly observed at 2.83, and 2.87 ppm, respectively and theoretically calculated value found at 3.65, 3.63, and 4.24 ppm, respectively. The molecule can readily release electrons for interaction with the macromolecule due to the increased energy of the HOMO. Consequently, the biological activity of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids increases with rising E HOMO values and decreases with rising E LUMO values. Another important factor in understanding the biological reactivity spectrum is E GAP . Lower E GAP values align with a wider range of biological reactivity 84 . The hard-soft-acid-base model is used to determine the coordination affinities of molecular systems. Soft acids tend to form coordination bonds with soft bases, while hard acids tend to form coordination bonds with hard bases 45 , 46 . Cells, enzymes, and other macro-molecules inherently possess a pliable nature, making it easier for the macromolecule to interact with soft molecules compared to hard ones. Higher softness values and lower hardness values indicate stronger interaction potential in molecular systems. The hypothesis that electrons can readily spread out inside the molecular system is supported by the elevated values of CP and the diminished values of relevant QCDs, including the nucleophilicity and electrophilicity indices. The HOMO and LUMO values are directly related to ionization and electron affinity potentials. HOMO values indicate the ability of a molecule to donate electrons, while LUMO values suggest the capacity to receive electrons. These values also reflect the structural stability of the molecular system. The red and green colours in the contour plot of HOMO and LUMO signify negative and positive charges, respectively 80 . The HOMO electrons are predominantly delocalized around the phenothiazine and imidazole rings, with small balloon-shaped electron clouds visible above the nitrogen and sulphur atoms of phenothiazine, suggesting these atoms are active and involved in molecular interactions. The LUMO electrons, on the other hand, are mostly delocalized over the resonating structure. The chemical stability and reactivity of the synthesised molecules can be indirectly measured by the energy gap. The compound’s low energy gap value makes it reactive and unstable 85 . Examining the effects of replacements on HOMO and LUMO values in contour diagrams reveals minimal variation, though substitutions can cause fluctuations in these values 83 . Table 6 provides the insights of the HOMO values of N -IPTZ(a-c) as − 5.63, − 5.62, and − 5.62 eV respectively, while the LUMO values as − 0.81, − 0.82, and − 1.04 eV respectively. It has been calculated that the energy gaps in the gas phase are 4.82, 5.54, and 4.58 eV respectively for the synthesised hybrids which suggests the reactivity order in the sequence as follows: N -IPTZ(c) > N -IPTZ(a) > N -IPTZ(b). It shows that N -IPTZ(c) is the most reactive and bioactive hybrid among other synthesised hybrid according to the QCD calculations and FMO plots 41 . It also ascertains whether a segment of the molecule exhibits attraction or repulsion towards a proton situated at any position surrounding the molecule. It distinguishes between electrophilic and nucleophilic sites by depicting them in red and blue, respectively, indicating the polarity of the molecule 84 . ChemDraw, OSIRIS Explorer, and Molinspiration are computational tools that can be used to evaluate the MDs, which plays an essential role in finding out bioavailable and drug-like chemical scaffolds. Several significant MD parameters include mi LogP, TPSA, MW (molecular weight), nAtom (number of atoms), and nArom (number of aromatic atoms). The most important parameters to look at for bioavailable, drug-likeness, and oral bioavailability are the number of atoms, number of aromatic atoms, fCsp3 (fraction Csp3), SA (synthetic accessibility), MR (molar refractivity), and percentage absorption. Moreover, MDs can be employed to enhance the screening of compounds from a library of compounds. The logP, which shows the partition coefficient between octanol and water, will likely be used to figure out the fragment-based support and correction factor. The topological polar surface area (TPSA) is commonly used to assess the pharmacokinetics of drugs, including their absorption in the gastrointestinal tract and their ability to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB). The number of atoms, the number of aromatic atoms, and the molecular weight of a molecular system are important parameters in influencing its ability to be transported and its suitability for biological functions. The number of hydrogen bond acceptors (nON) and the number of hydrogen bond donors (nOHNH) are essential factors that have the potential to impact the interaction of biological systems. The presence of numerous rotatable bonds, which are closely related to the molecule’s flexibility, has a significant impact on the oral bioavailability of a chemical system. The metric of SA is employed to assess the complexity of molecular synthesis. It provides details regarding the complexity and practicality of synthesising the chemical. One technique for evaluating the interaction characteristics inside the target-receptor complex is called molecular recognition (MR). The determination of molecules’ oral bioavailability also relies on the measurement of absorption percentage. To speed up the procedure of finding the most suitable biological target, the ligands’ molecular structure and oral bioavailability were investigated. As per display in Table 8 , all the synthesised N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids meet specific filtering requirements and exhibit acceptable oral bioavailability. THIO and DACA, two well-known conventional anticancer drugs, were used to correlate the results. Also, both standard drugs THIO and DACA were used as reference drug to synthesise N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids. The MD values of synthesised hybrids were within the normal range for drugs as markedly. For example, their molar refractivity values were 95.29, 100.25, and 124.52; synthetic accessibility values were 3.50, 3.62, and 3.93; and percentage absorption, which explains intestinal absorption, near about 90%. Synthesised hybrids, N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids, have shown similar bioavailability as standard drugs. Based on the rules of Lipinski, Veber, Ghose, Muegge, and lead-likeness filtering, Table 9 shows that the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids and standard drugs both have good oral bioavailability and absorption. In this way, these observations help N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids to cross the first drug-likeness stage and gives them permission for further evaluation. The assessment of oral bioavailability for a chemical agent is significantly influenced by ADMET properties. A chemical must satisfy the ADMET parameters to be considered as a drug, as clinical studies cannot commence until the assessment of ADMET properties is finalised. All synthesised hybrids N -IPTZ(a–c) were evaluated for ADMET parameters in the same manner as standard drugs (Erlotinib 14 , DOXO 11 , DACA 6 , and THIO 11 ) were assessed for their ADMET properties. This finding determined the oral bioavailability of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids. This was achieved by assessing the permeability of the BBB, the permeability of Caco-2, the absorption efficiency of drugs by the HIA, the distribution uniformity of plasma proteins, and their solubility in water. The absorption studies revealed HIA values for the synthesised hybrids N -IPTZ(a-c) of 0.973, 0.995, and 0.797, respectively. The values were favourably compared with the HIA values of standard drugs, including Erlonitib (0.935), DOXO (0.566), DACA (0.992), and THIO (0.906). Obtained results demonstrated a favourable correlation with HIA. BBB penetration for all the synthesised N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids was found at 0.962, 0.939, and 0.889, in comparison to the standard drugs (Erlotinib, DOXO, DACA, and THIO), which exhibited values of 0.937, 0.983, 0.938, and 0.990, respectively. Also, the Caco-2 cell permeability of all N -IPTZ(a–c) hybrids was about the same, at 0.580, 0.625, and 0.599. Standard drugs and reference compounds (I-VII) 13 – 17 had values between 0.528 and 0.791 and 0.500 and 0.688, respectively. This study anticipated two significant isoforms of cytochrome P450 enzymes, CYP3A4 and CYP2D6, which play significant roles in the metabolism and excretion. The results indicate that N -IPTZ(a-c) has a probability of 0.692, 0.624, and 0.572 for the CYP3A4 substrate, and 0.777, 0.672, and 0.743 for the CYP3A4 inhibitor. The results show that N -IPTZ(c) does not bind to CYP2D6, CYP2C9, or CYP3A4. Instead, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP2C19 work as inhibitors, while CYP3A4 does not work as an inhibitor. In the same way, we found that N -IPTZ(a-b) did not bind to CYP2C9, CYP2D6, or CYP3A4. However, CYP1A2 and CYP2D6 did, while CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 did not. N -IPTZ(b) showed CYP2C19 inhibition, whereas N -IPTZ(a) showed non-inhibition for CYP2C19. Furthermore, N -IPTZ(c) is an inhibitor of CYP2C19 and CYP1A2, with respective values of 0.723 and 0.834. All synthesised hybrids and reference compounds (I-VII) demonstrate both substrate and inhibitor properties for CYP3A4. The P-glycoprotein enzyme facilitates the removal of toxins and xenobiotics from cells. N -IPTZ(a-b) were identified as P-gp inhibitors and substrates, indicating that the introduction of these hybrids into the human system may hinder their efficient expulsion by the P-gp enzyme. 37 , 86 Toxicity is an essential parameter in the design and development of drug entities. This study examined toxicity parameters, such as organ, genomic, and ecotoxicity. As part of the organ toxicity, the drug can damage the liver, inhibit the hERG gene from working, irritate and corrode the eyes, hurt the lungs, harm the reproductive system, and cause acute oral toxicity. Additionally, the drug can cause genomic toxicity, which includes Ames’s mutagenesis, binary carcinogenesis, and the micronucleus assay. Similarly, ecotoxicity includes honeybee toxicity, biodegradation, and tetrahymena pyriformis toxicity (pIGC 50 ) 87 . Several toxicity metrics were forecast to evaluate the safety characteristics of these compounds. The AMES test is commonly utilised to determine a compound’s mutagenicity. A positive test implies that the compound is mutagenic. The absence of AMES toxicity in these hybrids has advantages over competing materials. None of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids exhibited any toxicity in the AMES mutagenicity test or carcinogenicity. All newly synthesised hybrids, when compared to standard drugs and reference molecules, showed no toxicity effects in N -IPTZ(a-c). Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) cells are an important epithelial cell line used in biomedical research. They explain cell polarity, cell adhesion, and drug transport. This study conducted by PreADMET analysis on MDCK cells, revealing permeability coefficient values of 136.723, 127.720, and 69.070 of N -IPTZ(a-c). These values indicate moderate permeability, which is vital for evaluating potential drug candidates’ absorption and bioavailability 72 . There were no problems with eye corrosion, eye irritation, AMES’s mutagenesis, or carcinogenesis with any of the synthesised hybrids. The only problems were with mild respiratory toxicity, reproductive toxicity, acute oral toxicity, micronucleus assay, and tetrahymena pyriformis toxicity (pIGC 50 ). We have also compared the ADMET properties of the synthesised hybrids N -IPTZ(a–c) to standard drugs like DOXO 11 , THIO 11 , DACA 6 , and erlotinib 14 , as well as the reference compound (I-VII) 13 – 17 . Based on the ADMET analysis, the newly synthesized hybrids had favourable pharmacokinetic properties. The results indicate that N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids have been found suitable for further research and analysis (Table 11 ). An all-atom molecular dynamic simulation investigation was conducted to effectively validate the stability of the projected docked ligand-EGFR complex. Implementing such a study would yield significant insights into the dynamic behaviour of both the ligand and the EGFR protein, as well as assess the ligand’s significant binding interactions with essential catalytic site residues 90 , 91 . After the docking calculations, a molecular dynamics simulation was carried out at 100 ns using the GROMACS programme version 2022.3. Based on their binding affinity, the best protein-ligand complex was chosen. The stability profile of N -IPTZ(c) hybrid in conjunction with the EGFR was assessed utilizing the GROMACS command line tool gmx_rmsd to evaluate their corresponding RMSD values during the simulation runs. RMSD indicates the degree of divergence for a collection of atoms (protein, ligand, or ligand–protein complex) from the corresponding original reference structure 92 . Elevated RMSD readings would correlate with considerable instability, associated with alterations in the structure of the examined molecule. RMSD quantifies the mean deviation of atomic locations in a ligand relative to a target over time. Reduced RMSD values signify enhanced stability, as the structure consistently aligns more closely with the target during the simulation. The average RMSD values during the plateau MD simulation interval (60–70 ns) were notably lower. Nevertheless, ligands converge around RMSD values (about 2.500 Å), where only the ligand–protein trajectories remained stable until the conclusion of the MD simulation at 100 ns. All subsequent observations indicate sustained binding of N -IPTZ(c) inside the EGFR binding site. To provide further insights into the stability of the complex binding site, the per residue root-mean-square fluctuation (RMSF) profile was calculated for ligand-bound protein. RMSF quantifies the average deviation of each atom from its mean position throughout the simulation. Reduced RMSF values signify enhanced stability, since the atoms or residues have less variation from their mean positions, indicating a more rigid and stable structure. The backbone RMSF of each protein was calculated using the GROMACS “gmx_rmsf” command line. This flexibility validation criterion offers insights into the role of protein residues in the structural variations of the ligand/protein complex. RMSF quantifies the temporal regression of the mean deviation for each residue from its reference point inside the minimised initial structures 93 . Positive RMSF values assigned to the residue range up on N -IPTZ(c) binding suggesting the great impact of these residues on N -IPTZ(c) EGFR binding. The Radius of gyration (R g ) can be determined by trajectory analysis methods that monitor atomic positions throughout time. The protein-ligand module of GROMACS was utilised to produce each combination for a molecular dynamics study. The ligand molecule was enhanced by directly adding hydrogen atoms using Avogadro software 1.2.0. The technique was repeated for each of the buildings. The protein-ligand complex with the lowest energy was optimised in both the NVT and NPT ensembles using default parameters. The steepest descent approach was employed to identify the protein-ligand complex with the lowest energy. In MD simulations, hydrogen bonds are essential for stabilising structures. The formation and dissociation of these connections occur as the protein undergoes deformation, considerably influencing overall stability. MD simulations can evaluate hydrogen bond formation and longevity, offering information into the system’s stability. The structure reached convergence after 1089 steps, with a force tolerance set at 1000 kJ/mol. The production run lasted 100 ns, and GMX instructions were utilised to analyse the RMSD, hydrogen bonds, and contact energy. An interaction plot in two dimensions was develop for the post-molecular dynamic’s simulation. The EGFR complex had the highest binding affinity and stability among all complexes (Fig. 11 ). The synthesised hybrid N -IPTZ(c) exhibited a binding energy of − 7.67 kcal/mol, which is more potent than that of the reference compound (III) 14 . Additionally, it demonstrated comparable binding energies with reference compounds (IV) 15 , DOXO 11 , and THIO 11 . Molecular dynamics simulations confirmed the stable binding of the N -IPTZ(c) protein to EGFR, indicating positive RMSF values at 100 ns. So, the N -IPTZ(c) hybrid showed attachment to the active site amino acid residues of EGFR, showing that it can positively inhibit EGFR. In this study, we have opted for the MM/PBSA approach to rescore complexes due to its faster computation of binding free energy compared to other force field-based methods such as free energy perturbation (FEP) or thermodynamic integration (TI) methods. The MM/PBSA calculations were performed using the gmx_mmpbsa program, focusing on the target protein-ligand complex. This method provides a more precise assessment of ligand-protein affinity than the most advanced flexible molecular docking techniques. Over a 100 ns simulation, the ligand exhibited a binding free energy (ΔG_binding) as − 21.78 kcal/mol. This analysis included the molecular mechanics internal energy (ΔE_internal), the sum of electrostatic and polar solvation energies (ΔE_electrostatic + ΔG_sol) and van der Waals interactions (ΔE_VDW) between the protein and ligand. This study utilised various concentrations of the synthesised hybrids ( N -IPTZ-a-c) on the HepG2 cell line, employing the MTT assay. 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), reference anti-cancer drug was used as positive control, and the results are expressed in IC 50 (µg/mL). Cells received treatments of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µg/mL for a duration of 24 h. Experiments were conducted in triplicate, and IC 50 values for each fraction were determined. N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids exhibited the statistically significant ( p ≤ 0.05) antitumor activity against HepG2 cells, with IC 50 values found at 50.51, 43.97, and 35.3 µg/mL, respectively. The results indicated that the N -IPTZ(c) hybrid demonstrated moderate anticancer activity against HepG2 cells, as evidenced by their IC 50 value (35.3 µg/mL) that is comparable to the IC 50 of positive control (5-FU), 27.49 µg/mL, which reflected significant cytotoxic inhibition in these cells, positioning it as the most effective hybrid as compared to others hybrid compounds (Fig. 12 ). Furthermore, we used IC 50 values to compare the inhibition of EGFR and HepG2 cells with reported reference compounds (I–VII) 13 – 17 , standard drugs (DOXO 11 , THIO 11 , and erlotinib 14 ), and N -IPTZ(c) hybrid. In this study, we designed and synthesised three N -IPTZ(a–c) hybrids and characterised by utilising ESI-MS, FT-IR, and ( 1 H and 13 C) NMR spectroscopic techniques. The theoretical calculations were performed using the DFT-B3LYP/6-311G(d, p) method. To optimize the structure of the N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids, DFT calculations at the 6-311G(d, p) level of theory were conducted in the gas phase. The structural parameters of the N -IPTZ hybrids were compared with the experimental FT-IR and NMR spectral data. The HOMO and LUMO values were used to confirm charge transfers within the N -IPTZ hybrid molecules to calculate E gap between them for knowing the stability of the synthesised hybrids. The calculated interaction energies of the N -IPTZs did not exhibit significant changes. Based on the HOMO and LUMO values, the N -IPTZ(c) was determined to be the most reactive N -IPTZ hybrid among all the synthesized hybrids, further supported by estimates of QCDs in the gas phase. In silico analysis were also done to observe their interaction profile with apoptosis target proteins in which thioridazine, dacarbazine, and doxorubicin were used as standard drugs. Further, the comparative bioavailability of all the synthesised hybrids along with standard drugs is also evaluated significantly. After subsequent screening, N -IPTZ(c) hybrids were identified as the promising anticancer candidates based on their binding energy and compatibility with drug-likeness parameters. We also used NVT, NPT, radius of gyration, RMSD, and RMSF studies to evaluate the stability and protein ligand binding interactions of the N -IPTZ(c) hybrid complex. The designed and synthesised compound exhibited inhibitory characteristics with lower toxicity levels and obeyed bioavailability parameters against anticancer receptors proteins. Molecular docking analysis showed that N -IPTZ(c) hybrids might be able to potentially bind with the amino acid residues (GLY695, SER696, GLY697, ALA698, PHE699, LYS721, GLY772, CYS773, THR766, GLN767, LEU768, MET769, ARG817, ASN818, and THR830, respectively) in the active pockets of EGFR. N -IPTZ(c) hybrids had lower binding energies than other synthesised hybrids and met the ADMET criteria without harming organs, causing cancer, mutations, or killing cells. Additionally, eco-toxicology evaluations considered N -IPTZ(a-c) hybrids as non-harmful candidate to fish and daphnia populations. All the synthesised hybrids were evaluated for their anti-cancer activity against human cancer cell line HepG2. Among all the synthesised hybrids, N -IPTZ(c) was found as the most effective candidate than N -IPTZ(a) and N -IPTZ(b) which exhibited notable anticancer activity (IC 50 , 35.3 µg/mL) comparable to positive control 5-FU (IC 50 , 27.49 µg/mL). Therefore, as per the existing compounds which are showing EGFR inhibition and HepG2 cell line inhibition, hybrid N -IPTZ(c) can be considered as moderate anticancer agent. articles.title: EGFR and CYP signaling disruption underlies 6PPD-quinone hepatotoxicity: Insights from a network and machine learning approach.
articles.abstract: N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine quinone (6PPD-quinone), an emerging environmental contaminant derived from the common tire additive 6PPD, has been alarmingly detected in human biology samples and is associated with liver injury. However, the underlying molecular mechanisms driving its hepatotoxicity remain largely unexplored. We employed an integrated in silico strategy encompassing ADMET profiling, network toxicology analysis, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and machine learning (ML) to uncover the hepatotoxic mechanisms of 6PPD-quinone. Our analysis identified 62 critical intersection genes between 6PPD-quinone targets and hepatotoxicity-related genes. Protein-protein interaction analysis revealed two distinct functional modules: Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR)-mediated signaling pathways and Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme-driven metabolic processes. Molecular docking and MD simulations confirmed remarkably strong and stable binding interactions between 6PPD-quinone and EGFR. An advanced ML model further classified 6PPD-quinone as a potent EGFR inhibitor, achieving a validation accuracy of 0.90 and an F1-score of 0.90. Comprehensive ADMET and docking analyses additionally indicated multi-CYP enzyme inhibition capabilities. In conclusion, our findings suggest that 6PPD-quinone induces liver injury through a dual mechanism involving EGFR signaling suppression and CYP-mediated metabolic disruption. This work provides a critical mechanistic framework for assessing the hepatotoxic risks of tire-derived environmental pollutants and highlights potential intervention targets for mitigating their adverse health effects.
articles.authors: Wenjie Zhang; Banghua Wu; Chengbin Hu; Weifeng Rong; Yongshun Huang; Shijie Hu; Yiru Qin

articles.title: Novel 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives of naproxen targeting EGFR: Synthesis, molecular docking studies, and cytotoxic evaluation.
articles.abstract: The close association between inflammation and cancer inspired the synthesis of a series of 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives (compounds H4-A-F) of 6-methoxynaphtalene. The chemical structures of the new compounds were validated utilizing Fourier-transform infrared, proton nuclear magnetic resonance, and carbon-13 nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic techniques and CHN analysis. Computer-aided drug design methods were used to predict the compounds biological target, ADMET properties, toxicity, and to evaluate the molecular similarities between the design compounds and erlotinib, a standard epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor. The antiproliferative effects of the new compounds were evaluated by the 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide assay, cell cycle analysis, apoptosis detection by microscopy, quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction, and immunoblotting, and EGFR enzyme inhibition assay. In silico analysis of the new oxadiazole derivatives indicated that these compounds target EGFR, and that compounds H4-A, H4-B, H4-C, and H4-E show similar molecular properties to erlotinib. Additionally, the results indicated that none of the synthesized compounds are carcinogenic, and that compounds H4-A, H4-C, and H4-F are nontoxic. Compound H4-A showed the best-fit score against EGFR pharmacophore model, however, the in vitro studies indicated that compound H4-C was the most cytotoxic. Compound H4-C caused cytotoxicity in HCT-116 colorectal cancer cells by inducing both apoptosis and necrosis. Furthermore, compounds H4-D, H4-C, and H4-B had potent inhibitory effect on EGFR tyrosine kinase that was comparable to erlotinib. The findings of this inquiry offer a basis for further investigation into the differences between the synthesized compounds and erlotinib. However, additional testing will be needed to assess all of these differences and to identify the most promising compound for further research.
articles.authors: Hiba N Alsaad; Baan M Al-Jasani; Ammar A Razzak Mahmood; Lubna H Tahtamouni; Khaled M Saleh; Mai F AlSakhen; Sana I Kanaan; Salem R Yasin

articles.title: Synthesis, ADMET predictions, molecular docking studies, and in-vitro anticancer activity of some benzoxazines against A549 human lung cancer cells.
articles.abstract: OBJECTIVES: This study aims to synthesize a series of benzoxazines ( 1 - 5 ) to be examined as an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor by in-silico study. The overexpression of EGFR causes the growth of normal lung cells to become uncontrollable, which may lead to cancer formation. We also conducted the absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretions and toxicity (ADMET) properties evaluation and also examined in vitro anticancer assay on human lung cancer cells line, which is A549. METHODS: Benzoxazines ( 1 - 5 ) were synthesized by reacting anthranilic acid and benzoyl chlorides. The structures of the compounds were determined with 1 H, 13 C-NMR, HRMS, UV and FT-IR spectrometric methods. Prediction of ADMET was using online pkCSM, and the molecular docking studies were using MVD with EGFR-TKIs as the target (PDB ID: 1M17). In vitro assay of anticancer activity was performed by MTT assay. RESULTS: Compounds 1 - 5 were successfully synthesized in good yields (71-84%). The ADMET prediction showed that benzoxazines are able to be absorbed through GIT, metabolized by CYP 450, and not hepatotoxic. The title compounds have a greater Moldock Score than Erlotinib, and 3 has the highest activity against A549 compared with other benzoxazines, IC 50 =36.6 μg/mL. CONCLUSIONS: Compound ( 3 ) more active as anticancer against Human cancer cells line compared with other benzoxazines.
articles.journal: Journal of basic and clinical physiology and pharmacology
articles.authors: Melanny Ika Sulistyowaty; Retno Widyowati; Galih Satrio Putra; Tutuk Budiati; Katsuyoshi Matsunami
articles.full_text: Cancer is one of the most prevalent and lethal diseases worldwide, contributing to a significant number of deaths annually. Because current cancer therapy regimens lack selectivity and have many adverse effects, research into alternative treatment techniques is still important. For decades, scientists have continued to investigate potential new drug candidates for cancer treatment by exploring both naturally derived compounds and synthetic pharmaceutical agents produced in laboratory settings [ 1 , 2 ]. Compounds with a naphthoquinone (NQ) framework are known to show strong activity against human breast, liver, cervical, and gastric cancer cells by inducing apoptosis [ 3 , 4 , 5 ]. The primary mechanism underlying the anticancer effect of quinones is thought to be the triggering of the autophagy system [ 6 , 7 , 8 , 9 ] and the formation of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) [ 10 , 11 , 12 ], such as hydroxyl radicals [ 13 ]. Ansamycin antibiotics, substituted‐1,4‐NQ derivatives containing amino groups, represent a significant part of antibiotics with anticancer activity [ 14 , 15 ]. Many NQ compounds are toxic to various cancer cell lines due to properties of redox potential [ 16 , 17 , 18 , 19 ]. For example, it has been shown that some alkylamino quinone derivatives have a high cytotoxic effect against human colon, brain, and pancreatic cancer cells [ 20 ]. Ourworking group has investigated a variety of quinone compounds and their uses in anticancer research on quinone derivatives replaced with heterogroups [ 21 , 22 , 23 , 24 , 25 , 26 ]. Furthermore, we discovered that 2‐( tert ‐butylthio)‐3‐chloronaphthalene‐1,4‐dione, one of the NQ derivatives with a sulfur atom substitution, had a strong antiproliferative effect on HeLa cells with an IC50 value of 10.16 µM [ 27 , 28 , 29 ]. We planned to direct our synthesis toward the discovery of novel N ‐, S ‐substituted quinone analogues and investigate their antiproliferative activities against human lung, breast, and prostate cancer cells and their cytotoxic activities on mouse embryonic fibroblast cells in this study. The molecular docking approach is a simulation technique used to predict potential interactions in drug development processes. This method allows for the determination of interactions and binding affinities between drug candidate molecules and their target receptors. Drug development efforts can benefit from generating a prediction profile by analyzing the interactions between disease‐related macromolecules and potential therapeutic compounds, providing valuable insights to complement experimental studies. Given the anticancer properties of 1,4‐NQ derivatives, molecular docking studies were conducted to explore the interactions between newly synthesized naphthalene‐1,4‐dione‐linked phenylpiperazine and thioether derivatives and various cancer‐related targets in this study. Moreover, the antimicrobial activity potential of quinone derivatives was determined by the agar well diffusion method. As well, the minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of quinone derivatives were determined. The reaction of 2,3‐dihalo(Cl)‐1,4‐NQ ( 1 ) with some nucleophiles ( 2a–d ) gave S,S‐ , N ‐, and N,S ‐substituted quinone analogues (Scheme 1 ). Both halogen atoms, such as chlorine and bromine, can be replaced by using nitrogen‐containing heterocycles [ 30 ]. The one strong nucleophile property reacts with 2,3‐dihalo‐1,4‐NQ; only one chlorine atom can be displaced due to the electronic density of the quinone system. The displacement of the second chlorine atom and its separation from the structure can be easily achieved if the quinone system contains an electron‐withdrawing group (EWG) or in the presence of ethanol [ 31 , 32 , 33 , 34 , 35 ]. The synthesis of 1,4‐NQs ( 3 – 10 ). The synthesis of 1,4‐NQ derivatives ( 3–10 ) was investigated by the reactions of 2,3‐dihalo‐1,4‐NQ (1) with 1‐(4‐chlorobenzhydryl)piperazine ( 2a ), hexanethiol ( 2b ), octanethiol ( 2c ), and 1‐[bis(4‐fluorophenyl)‐methyl]piperazine ( 2d ) (Scheme 1 ). In a previous study, heteroatom‐substituted NQ derivatives containing piperonylpiperazine or diphenylmethylpiperazine in Position two and chlorine atom in Position three of the 1,4‐NQ moiety showed good antiproliferative profiles and also antibacterial and antioxidant capacity. In the literature [ 31 ], the compounds of 2‐( N ‐diphenylmethylpiperazin‐1‐yl)‐3‐chloro‐1,4‐NQ and 2‐[1‐piperonylpiperazin‐1‐yl]‐3‐chloro‐1,4‐NQ showed a strong cytotoxic effect on MCF‐7 cell lines and significant antibacterial activity against Micrococcus luteum . Interestingly, they also have powerful antioxidant capacity [ 31 ]. In this study, a similar compound, 2‐(4‐(bis(3‐fluorophenyl)methyl)piperazin‐1‐yl)‐3‐chloronaphthalene‐1,4‐dione, also gave similar results. The strong antimicrobial activity demonstrated by our similar compounds has encouraged us to further our studies on this subject. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , when starting compound ( 1 ) reacted in a single reaction vessel with an equimolar amount of various amines and thiols ( 2a–d ). The products ( 4 and 7 – 9 ) were synthesized in good yields with appropriate reaction times and high purity. In the FT‐IR spectra of newly synthesized compounds, the stretching bands for the carbonyl group gave peaks in the range of 1537–1730 cm −1 . According to 13 C(APT) NMR spectrum, except for synthesized quinone compounds ( 4 , 5 , and 7–10 ), two carbonyl signals were given between 178.05 and 182.90 ppm. It was proved that two different substituents were connected to the quinone unit. However, the individual carbonyl carbons (C═O) of compounds 3 and 6 provided single chemical shift values at δ 178.94 and 178.98 ppm, respectively. These 13 C(APT) shift values have agreed well with the related literature [ 36 , 37 , 38 , 39 ]. The respective molecular ion peaks were observed at m / z (%) m / z = 391.28329 [M] + (compound 3 ), 559.21674 [M + H] + (compound 4 ), 477.11203 [M] + (compound 5 ), 447.23743 [M] + (compound 6 ), 587.24829 [M] + (compound 7 ), 561.23718 [M] + (compound 8 ), 589.26807 [M] + (compound 9 ), and 479.13260 [M + H] + (compound 10 ), respectively, for the synthesized compounds ( 3 – 10) by using the ESI method of high‐resolution mass spectrum (HRMS) method. The antiproliferative effect of the synthesized compounds ( 3 – 10 ) on human cancer cell lines was screened at a concentration of 10 µM using the 3‐(4,5‐dimethylthiazol‐2‐yl)‐2,5‐diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The effect of the compounds on the cell viability at 24 h is shown in Figure 1 . The cytotoxic effects of the synthesized compounds were also assessed in NIH/3T3 cells. According to the results, compound 10 was determined to be the most effective compound against all cancer cell lines in this study. The highest inhibitory activity of compound 2‐(4‐(Bis(3‐Fluorophenyl)Methyl)Piperazin‐1‐yl)‐3‐Chloronaphthalene‐1,4‐Dion) 10 was observed in PC‐3 cells (51.66%). Additionally, compound 10 has low toxicity toward healthy cells (10.1%), indicating that it does not significantly harm NIH/3T3 cells. Interestingly, compound 10 showed a stronger inhibition on cancer cell proliferation than the reference drug doxorubicin (DOX), which contains a 1,4‐NQ ring. On the other hand, the compounds 5 and 7 showed promising results against A549 cells (36.17% and 36.71%, respectively); however, the remaining compounds at 10 µM concentration had virtually no antiproliferative effects. The effect of compounds on the viability of cancer cell lines and NIH/3T3 cells ( n = 3). DOX, doxorubicin. The fact that compound 10 has an effect on all three cancer cell lines is due to the fluorophenyl piperazine ring it contains. The biological effects of piperazine‐conjugated molecules might vary greatly, with the main one being anticancer action [ 40 , 41 ]. Consistent with our results, a very recent study by Zsoldos et al. reported that 1‐bis(4‐fluorophenyl)methylpiperazine derivatives exhibited potent anticarcinogenic activity on all cancer cell lines used in the study [ 42 ]. On the basis of the results of the MTT experiment, docking was carried out among the three compounds ( 5 , 7 , and 10 ) that gave the highest cytotoxic activity in all three cancer cell lines and the target proteins. The selection of target proteins for molecular docking studies was based on their biological roles in cancer signaling pathways as supported by the literature [ 43 , 44 , 45 ]. The cell lines utilized in this study (A549, PC‐3, and MCF‐7) serve as model systems for lung, prostate, and breast cancers, respectively. For these cell lines, proteins such as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2 (VEGFR2), mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), Caspase‐3, focal adhesion kinase (FAK), and cyclin‐dependent kinase 6 (CDK6) were selected due to their critical roles in the biology of the respective cancer types, as highlighted in previous studies. Comparison with docetaxel, which is used as a positive control, reveals that three synthesized quinone compounds ( 5 , 7 , and 10 ) exhibit significantly higher binding scores, as depicted in Table 2 . Simultaneously with the cytotoxic activity results, we observed that the molecule coded as compound 10 demonstrates excellent docking scores against all proteins. Furthermore, the highest score (−9.8 kcal/mol), as indicated in Table 1 , is obtained from the docking experiment of the compound 10 molecule with the EGFR protein. Abbreviations: CDK6, cyclin‐dependent kinase 6; EGFR, epidermal growth factor receptor; FAK, focal adhesion kinase; mTOR, mammalian target of rapamycin; VEGFR2, vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2. The overexpression or mutation of EGFR is often associated with the poor prognosis, rapid metastasis, short‐term recurrence, and short survival time of epithelium tumors, such as breast, gastrointestinal, ovarian, prostate, and cervical cancer [ 46 ]. Increasing evidence has shown that EGFR inhibitors have great potential in cancer treatment, especially for non‐small cell lung cancer, colon cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma, which has led to an increasing number of research studies based on the design and synthesis of EGFR inhibitors [ 43 , 47 ]. NQs have been shown to be involved in various molecular processes that drive cancer cells to enter the apoptotic process, such as suppression of the EGFR–NF‐κB signaling pathway [ 48 ]. Furthermore, many studies have shown that 1,4‐NQ derivatives exhibit strong effects on various cancer cells as potential EGFR inhibitors [ 19 , 29 , 49 ]. Overexpression of EGFR activates pro‐oncogenic downstream signaling pathways, including PI3K/AKT/mTOR [ 50 , 51 ]. This signaling plays key roles in oncogenesis‐related events, such as cell survival, cell proliferation, cell differentiation, and cellular apoptosis. In vitro studies have shown that PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling plays an important role not only in the proliferation and apoptosis of prostate cancer cells [ 52 ] but also in migration and invasion [ 53 ]. In our study, compound 10 exhibits the strongest inhibitory effect on prostate cancer cells and shows a strong binding with mTOR, suggesting that this compound may be an alternative potential therapeutic agent for prostate cancer treatment by inhibiting PI3/AKT/mTOR signaling. Analyzing the protein–ligand interaction of the positive control and the compound 10 molecule when docked with EGFR, considering the numerical data, both molecules exhibit high‐density hydrophobic interactions with similar amino acids (Figure 2a,b ). Moreover, it is noteworthy that both molecules position themselves in a comparable location within the active region of the protein (Figure 2c,d ). The interaction between docetaxel and EGFR (a) and docetaxel and compound 10 (b); 3D visualization of the molecular interaction between docetaxel and EGFR (c) and docetaxel and compound 10 (d). In conclusion, the docking studies were found to be in agreement with the in vitro antiproliferative data, particularly for compound 10 . The observed interactions between compound 10 and EGFR suggest a possible mechanism of action, which may help guide future experimental investigations. In the present study, compounds 5 and 8 were effective against all of the microorganisms, both bacteria and yeast. Compound 7 was effective against all of the microorganisms except Escherichia coli ATCC 25922, and compound 10 , a derivative, was effective against all of the microorganisms except Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 43300 (Table 3 ). Moreover, compound 3 was effective against S. aureus ATCC 25923 (Figure 3 ), S. aureus ATCC 43300, Staphylococcus epidermidis ATCC 11228, Klebsiella pneumoniae ATCC 4352, and Candida albicans ATCC 90028. Compounds 4 and 9 showed similar activities as being effective against S. aureus ATCC 43300, S. epidermidis ATCC 11228, Pseudomonas aeruginosa ATCC 27853, and K. pneumoniae ATCC 4352 (Table 2 ). In their study, the authors declared that synthesized compounds (3 – 5 and 7 – 10 ) showed antibacterial effects against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa . Additionally, it was stated that the five synthesized compounds ( 3 – 5 , 7 , and 9 ) in our study have shown antifungal effects (Figure 4 ) against C. albicans at various concentrations of MIC (Table 3 ). As it is known, quinone can be transformed to detrimental ROS by catalyzing redox cycles where NAP(P)H and molecular O 2 take place. Moreover, some quinone derivatives have the ability to show their antiproliferative activity by impairing mitochondrial functions not only in bacteria (such as S. aureus ) but also in fungi and protozoa [ 57 ]. In order to find out a solution for new agents against the antimicrobial resistance, Ulfah et al. have shown that diterpene quinone–derived compounds are safe to use. The investigators have stated that in vitro assay results of their study had shown that the antimicrobial activities of diterpene quinone–derived compounds were related to the inhibition of ATP as a function and disruption of the membrane function of S. aureus . Additionally, diterpene quinone–derived compounds exhibited good antibacterial activity. On the basis of in vitro and in silico assays, the mechanism of action of this compound is related to the disruption of bacterial membrane function, and it has the potential to inhibit the ATPase enzyme and the S. aureus gyrase [ 58 ]. In our opinion, the synthesized naphthalene‐1,4‐diones in our study (naphthalene‐1,4‐dione‐linked phenyl piperazine and thioether) might show efficacy against microorganisms by detrimental ROS by catalyzing redox cycles and impairing mitochondrial functions and bacterial membrane function and inhibiting the ATPase enzyme. The new quinone analogues ( 3–10 ) were synthesized by the reaction of 1,4‐NQ ( 1 ) with heterocyclic ring‐containing nucleophiles such as thiols and piperazine derivatives in this study. The chemical structures of these newly synthesized compounds were elucidated by FT‐IR, 1 H NMR, 13 C(APT) NMR, and HRMS. In the past study, some synthetic NQ derivatives showed good antiproliferative profiles and also antibacterial and antioxidant capacity. The strong antimicrobial activity demonstrated by our similar compounds has encouraged us to further our studies on this subject. The compounds were assessed for their ability to inhibit the growth of different cancer cell lines (MCF‐7, A549, PC‐3) and NIH/3T3 using the MTT assay. Compound 10 exhibited potent antiproliferative effects on various cancer cell lines, notably PC‐3, with minimal cytotoxicity toward NIH/3T3 cells. However, compounds 5 and 7 showed promising activity against A549 cells. These compounds offer the potential to treat cancer, and their different biological effects should be investigated in future studies. Our molecular docking analysis revealed favorable binding affinities for compound 10 , particularly with EGFR, in alignment with its observed in vitro cytotoxicity. Although these findings are promising, they should be interpreted as preliminary insights that require further biological validation to confirm their relevance. These findings corroborate previous studies, which also employed docking methods and reported high‐affinity binding of quinone derivatives to EGFR. The potential interaction between quinone‐based compounds and EGFR has been previously explored in silico. For instance, Nafie et al. reported that quinoline‐based thiazolidinone derivatives exhibited significant binding affinity toward EGFR, suggesting their potential as anticancer agents. Similarly, Mohanty et al. conducted computational studies on quinazoline derivatives, highlighting their favorable interactions with the EGFR active site. These findings align with our docking results, further supporting the hypothesis that quinone derivatives may serve as effective EGFR inhibitors [ 59 , 60 ]. Collectively, our findings indicate that quinone derivatives, especially compound 10 , may have potential as anticancer agents targeting EGFR; however, further in vitro and in vivo studies are necessary to validate this hypothesis. Further studies are warranted to validate these findings in vivo and explore the molecular mechanisms underlying compound 10 ’s anticancer activity. We have investigated the antimicrobial effects of various quinone derivatives, which we synthesized. The findings we have mentioned above are quite important, as the new quinone derivatives that we have synthesized have shown various antimicrobial effects against both important gram‐positive and gram‐negative bacteria, together with antifungal effect. Hence, these compounds show activity against various microorganisms that are human pathogens; considering this antimicrobial activity, our study may have potential for drug development. Accordingly, our study can be quite beneficial because it might shed light for future studies on this area. Melting points were measured by using a Büchi B‐540 melting point apparatus. NIH/3T3, MCF‐7, and A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/F12 (DMEM/F12), whereas PC‐3 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco, Rockville, MD, USA). All used media contain 10% v/v fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% v/v penicillin‐streptomycin (Gibco, Rockville, MD, USA). Cells were grown in a humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO 2 . In our study, DOX, which is one of the most well‐known antineoplastic drugs, was also used as a positive control in this method. The MTT assay (Vybrant MTT Cell Proliferation Assay Kit, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was utilized to investigate the antiproliferative and cytotoxic effects of the compounds [ 64 ]. The cells were seeded in a 96‐well plate (1 × 10 4 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h. The next day, the cells were treated with a 10 µM concentration of compounds for 24 h. At the end of the period, MTT solution was added to each well at a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL and incubated for an additional 4 h. Then, the purple formazan product was solubilized by adding 100 µL of SDS buffer. Absorption measurements were taken at wavelengths of 570 and 630 nm using a microplate reader (Biotech Instruments, Winooksi, VT, USA). Cell viability percentages were calculated with optical density. To conduct molecular docking studies for the cell lines A549, PC‐3, and MCF‐7 used in this study, target molecules were first determined in light of the literature. articles.title: Search for potentially biased epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors through pharmacophore modelling, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation approaches.
articles.abstract: Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), being one of the most crucial receptor in cancer therapy, has been selected as a potential target for the present study. Ligand-based pharmacophore model ( n = 30, R 2 =0.93 with root mean square deviation = 1.14, ΔCost = 144.27 and configuration cost = 21) was developed and validated with Fischer's randomisation (at 95% confidence), test set ( n = 225, R 2 pred = 0.81), external data set ( n = 13, R 2 pred = 0.95) and decoy set ( n = 70), further the model has been used to search for novel EGFR inhibitors. The validated model was used for virtual screening of zinc database. A pool of 115,948 candidate molecules was screened through the model. Subsequently, molecules having predicted IC 50 <0.2 µM were selected for screening through drug-like properties filter. Based on pharmacokinetic profile (ADMET study), Lipinski's rule of five and Veber's rule, 62 molecules were shortlisted for molecular docking. Using consensus docking, five hit molecules were selected, which were further considered for molecular dynamics simulation. Additionally MM-GBSA analysis was carried which showed that affinity of hits towards the receptor of three compound mainly ZINC305, ZINC131796 and ZINC131785 were similar to the standard vanedtinib. The simulation, performed for 100 ns, revealed that two hit molecules, namely ZINC305 and ZINC131785, showing potential interactions at the ligand-binding domain of EGFR protein with good ligand-protein stability. Communicated by Ramaswamy H. Sarma.
articles.authors: Megha Jethwa; Aditi Gangopadhyay; Achintya Saha
